walkthrough.title: Separate the Two Decks
walkthrough.text: Let's get Cards Against Humanity set up. Open the box and you will find two types of cards. The black cards have white text and are your prompts. The white cards have black text and are your answers. Separate them into two piles if they are not already sorted.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle Both Decks
walkthrough.text: Give each deck a thorough shuffle. The black deck has about one hundred cards and the white deck has about five hundred, so you may want to split the white deck in half and shuffle each half separately. Stack them back together when you are done.

walkthrough.title: Place the Decks on the Table
walkthrough.text: Put both decks face down in the center of the table where everyone can reach them. Leave some open space nearby for players to place their submissions face down each round. This is your submission area.

walkthrough.title: Deal White Cards
walkthrough.text: Deal ten white cards face down to each player. Everyone picks up their cards and looks at them, but keep them hidden from other players. These ten cards are your starting hand and you will draw back up to ten after every round.

walkthrough.title: Choose the First Card Czar
walkthrough.text: Pick someone to be the first Card Czar. You can choose randomly, pick the birthday closest to today, or use whatever silly method your group prefers. The Card Czar role rotates clockwise after every round, so everyone will get a turn judging.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play
walkthrough.text: That is it for setup. Each player should have ten white cards in hand, both decks should be shuffled and centered on the table, and you should know who the first Card Czar is. The Card Czar will kick things off by drawing the first black card.

summary.title: Separate the Two Decks
bullets: Black cards = prompts (white text on black)
White cards = answers (black text on white)
Separate into 2 face-down piles

summary.title: Shuffle Both Decks
bullets: Shuffle black deck (~100 cards)
Shuffle white deck (~500 cards) — split in half to shuffle easier

summary.title: Place the Decks on the Table
bullets: Both decks face-down in center of table
Leave open space for face-down submissions

summary.title: Deal White Cards
bullets: Deal 10 white cards to each player
Players look at own cards, keep hidden from others

summary.title: Choose the First Card Czar
bullets: Pick first Card Czar by any method
Role rotates clockwise each round

summary.title: Ready to Play
bullets: 10 white cards per player, decks centered, Czar chosen
Card Czar draws first black card to begin

walkthrough.title: The Card Czar Draws
walkthrough.text: Each round starts with the Card Czar drawing the top black card and reading it aloud to the group. Black cards have a fill-in-the-blank statement or a question. Read it clearly so everyone understands exactly what they need to respond to.

walkthrough.title: Check the Pick Count
walkthrough.text: Before everyone submits, check the bottom of the black card. Most black cards require one white card, but some say Pick Two or Pick Three. This tells every player how many white cards they need to submit this round.

walkthrough.title: Players Submit White Cards
walkthrough.text: Everyone except the Card Czar chooses white cards from their hand and places them face down in the submission area. For Pick Two or Pick Three cards, the order you stack your cards matters because they will be read in that order. Take a moment to think about which arrangement sounds funniest.

walkthrough.title: The Card Czar Reads Submissions
walkthrough.text: Once everyone has submitted, the Card Czar collects all the face-down submissions and shuffles them so nobody knows whose cards are whose. Then the Czar re-reads the black card and fills in each player's white card one at a time. Read every combination fully before picking a winner.

walkthrough.title: The Card Czar Picks a Winner
walkthrough.text: After reading all the combinations, the Card Czar picks whichever one they found funniest. The Czar's decision is final and there are no appeals. The winning player reveals themselves and takes the black card, placing it face up in front of them as one Awesome Point.

walkthrough.title: Draw Back Up
walkthrough.text: After the winner is chosen, every player who submitted white cards draws new ones from the white deck to bring their hand back up to ten. The Card Czar does not draw because they did not play any cards this round. All played white cards that did not win are discarded.

walkthrough.title: Rotate the Card Czar
walkthrough.text: The Card Czar role passes clockwise to the next player. The new Czar draws a black card, reads it aloud, and a new round begins. The previous Czar rejoins as a regular player and can now submit white cards again.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: Before you start, agree on a target score. Five to ten Awesome Points works well depending on group size. The first player to reach the target wins. You can also just play until the black cards run out or until your group decides to stop, and whoever has the most Awesome Points at that point is the winner.

summary.title: The Card Czar Draws
bullets: Czar draws top black card → reads aloud
Black card has fill-in-the-blank or question

summary.title: Check the Pick Count
bullets: Default = Pick 1 (one white card)
Some cards say Pick 2 or Pick 3 at the bottom

summary.title: Players Submit White Cards
bullets: All non-Czar players submit white card(s) face-down
Pick 2/3: order matters — first card fills first blank

summary.title: The Card Czar Reads Submissions
bullets: Czar collects and shuffles submissions (anonymity)
Re-reads black card with each submission inserted

summary.title: The Card Czar Picks a Winner
bullets: Czar picks funniest combo — decision is final
Winner takes the black card = 1 Awesome Point

summary.title: Draw Back Up
bullets: Players draw white cards back up to 10
Czar does not draw (did not play)
Non-winning submissions discarded

summary.title: Rotate the Card Czar
bullets: Czar passes clockwise to next player
New Czar draws black card → new round

summary.title: Winning the Game
bullets: Agree on target score before starting (5-10 pts typical)
First to target wins, or most pts when group stops

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Practice
walkthrough.text: We are going to walk through a few practice rounds with five example players. You will see the basic flow, watch some common mistakes happen, and learn what makes a winning play. Follow along even if your group is a different size.

walkthrough.title: Round One: The Czar Reads
walkthrough.text: They draw a black card and read it aloud: "What is Batman's guilty pleasure?" The other four players look through their ten white cards to find the funniest answer. Take your time and actually read the black card carefully before choosing.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Pure Shock Value
walkthrough.text: Player three has a really offensive card in their hand and immediately slaps it down without even thinking about how it connects to Batman. The card is shocking, sure, but it has nothing to do with the question. It is just gross for the sake of being gross. Meanwhile, player four picks a card that is mildly absurd but perfectly fits the Batman theme.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Fit Beats Shock
walkthrough.text: The Card Czar reads all four submissions. Player three's shock card gets a groan, but player four's thematic answer gets a huge laugh and wins the round. The lesson here is that context matters more than shock value. A card that perfectly answers the question will almost always beat a random offensive card that has nothing to do with the prompt.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: A Pick Two Card
walkthrough.text: They draw a black card that says "Step one: blank. Step two: blank. Step three: profit." This is a Pick Two card, so everyone needs to submit two white cards in the order they want them read. The order you place your cards in is the order the Czar will read them.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Wrong Card Order
walkthrough.text: Player five has two great cards but places them in the wrong order. When the Czar reads the combination, it makes no sense because the punchline comes before the setup. Player three, learning from last round, carefully arranges their two cards so the second one delivers the punchline. The reading order can make or break your combo.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Order Is Everything
walkthrough.text: For Pick Two and Pick Three cards, always read your combination out loud in your head before submitting. Place the setup card first and the punchline card second. Swapping the order of the same two cards can turn a winning answer into nonsense. Think of it like telling a joke: the punchline always comes last.

walkthrough.title: Round Three: Reading the Czar
walkthrough.text: They draw a black card and read it aloud. Now the remaining players need to think about what player three finds funny. Player one noticed that player three has laughed hardest at absurd and silly answers in the first two rounds, not at dark or mean-spirited ones.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Ignoring the Czar
walkthrough.text: Player five plays their most shocking card because they personally think it is hilarious. Player one plays something genuinely silly and absurd that they think will match player three's sense of humor. Player three reads all the submissions and, sure enough, picks player one's silly card as the winner. Player five is confused because they thought their card was funnier.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Play to the Czar
walkthrough.text: You are not trying to pick the card you think is funniest. You are trying to pick the card the current Card Czar will think is funniest. Pay attention to what makes each person laugh when they are judging. Some Czars love dark humor, some love absurdity, and some love clever wordplay. Adjusting your picks to the Czar is the single biggest skill in this game.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Hoarding Good Cards
walkthrough.text: Player four has been holding onto a universally hilarious white card for three rounds now, waiting for the perfect black card. The problem is that the perfect black card may never come. Meanwhile, player four keeps playing their worst cards and losing every round. Three rounds of weak plays is a lot of wasted opportunities.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Play Your Best Cards
walkthrough.text: Do not hoard your strongest cards waiting for a perfect match. If a card is pretty good for the current prompt, play it now. You will draw a new card to replace it, and that new card might be even better. Saving one amazing card while losing three rounds with duds is a losing strategy. Keep your hand rotating.

walkthrough.title: Being a Great Card Czar
walkthrough.text: When it is your turn to be the Card Czar, read the black card with energy. Then read every single submission dramatically, even the bad ones. Give each combo a fair shot by re-reading the full black card with each answer inserted. A great Czar makes the whole table laugh, and that is half the fun of the game.

walkthrough.title: Before You Play
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for more on reading the Czar and managing your hand.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to card types, roles, and popular house rules.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Read the Czar, Not the Room
walkthrough.text: The single most important skill in Cards Against Humanity is playing to the Card Czar, not to yourself. Every person has a different sense of humor. Some Czars love maximum absurdity, some prefer dark humor, and some go for clever wordplay every time. Pay attention during the first few rounds to figure out each person's pattern. When it is their turn to judge, play the card that matches their taste, not yours.

walkthrough.title: Variety Wins Over Repetition
walkthrough.text: Going for pure shock value works the first time, maybe the second. But by the third round of relentlessly offensive answers, the table is numb to it and nobody laughs anymore. Mix up your approach. Follow a dark answer with something silly. Follow something absurd with something unexpectedly wholesome. Keeping the table guessing makes your submissions funnier because nobody sees them coming.

walkthrough.title: Read the Black Card Carefully
walkthrough.text: This sounds obvious, but a surprising number of players glance at the black card and immediately reach for their most offensive white card. Slow down and actually read the prompt. Think about the sentence structure, the setup, and what kind of punchline would land best. A mediocre card that perfectly fits the blank will beat a great card that makes no grammatical sense in the sentence.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Hand
walkthrough.text: You always draw back up to ten cards, so there is no reason to hoard. If you have a strong card that fits the current prompt pretty well, play it now. Waiting three more rounds for the perfect prompt means three rounds of weak plays. Also, if your entire hand feels useless, consider using the Rebooting the Universe house rule to trade in an Awesome Point for ten fresh cards.

walkthrough.title: Master the Pick Two
walkthrough.text: Pick Two cards are where good players separate themselves from great ones. The order of your two white cards matters enormously. Always put the setup in the first blank and the punchline in the second blank. Read the full sentence out loud in your head before submitting. If the combo sounds funny in both orders, go with the arrangement that has the bigger laugh at the end.

walkthrough.title: Be an Entertaining Czar
walkthrough.text: When you are the Card Czar, you set the energy for the entire round. Read the black card with enthusiasm. Then read each submission dramatically, pausing before the punchline for effect. Re-read the full prompt with every answer, not just the white card by itself. A great Czar makes every round memorable, even when the answers are weak.

walkthrough.title: Use House Rules Wisely
walkthrough.text: Cards Against Humanity has several official house rules that can keep the game fresh. Rando Cardrissian adds a random white card each round, which keeps everyone on their toes. Rebooting the Universe lets you trade a point for a fresh hand when you are stuck. Never Have I Ever lets you swap out cards you do not understand. Talk with your group at the start about which house rules you want to use.

walkthrough.title: Keep the Game Fun
walkthrough.text: The real goal is to make people laugh, not just to win. If someone at the table is uncomfortable with certain types of humor, respect that and adjust. Pre-screening the deck to remove cards that cross the line for your group is totally fine. A table full of people laughing is better than a table where one person is having a bad time. The points are just an excuse to keep playing.

summary.title: Read the Czar, Not the Room
bullets: Play to the Czar's humor, not your own
Track each person's taste — dark, absurd, clever, wholesome
Adapt your picks every time the Czar rotates

summary.title: Variety Wins Over Repetition
bullets: Pure shock loses impact after 2-3 rounds
Mix dark → silly → absurd → wholesome
Unpredictability makes your answers funnier

summary.title: Read the Black Card Carefully
bullets: Read the full prompt, not just a glance
Consider sentence structure and what fits grammatically
A fitting mediocre card > a great card that doesn't fit

summary.title: Manage Your Hand
bullets: Don't hoard — you draw back to 10 every round
Play strong cards now, draw replacements later
Reboot the Universe house rule: trade 1 pt for 10 fresh cards

summary.title: Master the Pick Two
bullets: Order matters: setup card first, punchline card second
Read the full sentence in your head before submitting
Pick 2 is where good players separate from great ones

summary.title: Be an Entertaining Czar
bullets: Read black card with energy, pause before punchlines
Re-read full prompt with every submission
Great Czar = great round, even with weak answers

summary.title: Use House Rules Wisely
bullets: Rando Cardrissian — random card entry keeps everyone sharp
Rebooting the Universe — fresh hand when stuck (costs 1 pt)
Agree on house rules before starting

summary.title: Keep the Game Fun
bullets: Goal is laughter, not just winning
Pre-screen cards if needed for your group's comfort
A fun table > a competitive table

walkthrough.title: Czar Profiling
walkthrough.text: Advanced players build a mental profile of every Czar at the table. Track three things for each person: what category of humor makes them laugh the hardest, whether they prefer short punchy answers or elaborate setups, and whether they tend to pick the first big laugh or reconsider after hearing all submissions. By round three or four, you should have a reliable read on every player at the table.

walkthrough.title: The Meta Game
walkthrough.text: Experienced groups develop patterns. If the table has been picking dark answers all night, a suddenly wholesome or absurdly innocent submission will catch the Czar off guard and win. This is called playing the meta. Pay attention to what has been winning recently and deliberately go against the trend. The surprise factor alone can carry a mediocre card to victory.

walkthrough.title: Strategic Hand Cycling
walkthrough.text: Your hand refreshes every round, so treat weak cards as disposable. When you have a hand full of situational cards that do not fit the current prompt, play your worst card as a throwaway. This cycles it out and gives you a fresh draw. Over several rounds, this keeps your hand quality high. Never waste a strong card on a round you know you cannot win.

walkthrough.title: Pick Two Mastery
walkthrough.text: Pick Two cards reward players who think about narrative structure. The first card sets the scene and the second card delivers the punchline. Advanced players sometimes reverse this on purpose, using the second card as a deadpan anti-climax. Experiment with both structures and watch which one your current Czar responds to. The ability to craft a two-card joke is the biggest skill gap between casual and competitive players.

walkthrough.title: When to Reboot
walkthrough.text: The Rebooting the Universe house rule is a powerful tool if your group uses it. The right time to reboot is when you have zero playable cards and the next Czar is someone whose taste you understand well. Trading one Awesome Point for ten fresh cards can net you two or three points over the next several rounds. The wrong time to reboot is when you are already behind and cannot afford to lose a point.

walkthrough.title: Playing as the Underdog
walkthrough.text: When someone is running away with the game, the table often subconsciously gangs up on them by not picking their submissions. Use this dynamic to your advantage. If you are behind, play confidently and let the leader's answers get overlooked by annoyed Czars. If you are ahead, consider playing slightly less obvious cards so your writing style is harder to identify.

walkthrough.title: Anonymity Is Your Weapon
walkthrough.text: Submissions are anonymous for a reason. If the table starts recognizing your handwriting, card placement style, or the fact that you always submit last, they can bias against you. Vary when you submit your cards. Avoid reacting to your own submission when the Czar reads it. Advanced players maintain a poker face so the Czar judges the answer on its own merit, not on who played it.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Awareness
walkthrough.text: When players are one or two points from winning, the dynamic shifts. Czars may become more reluctant to give the leader another point. Use this to your advantage by flying under the radar as you approach the finish line. If you are tied for the lead, play your absolute best card for the current Czar because tiebreaker rounds are unpredictable. Save your universally strong cards for these final critical rounds.

summary.title: Czar Profiling
bullets: Track 3 things per Czar: humor type, answer length pref, picking pattern
Build profiles over first 3-4 rounds
Reliable Czar reads = consistent wins

summary.title: The Meta Game
bullets: Track what's been winning recently
Deliberately go against the trend for surprise factor
Wholesome answer after a string of dark ones = unexpected win

summary.title: Strategic Hand Cycling
bullets: Play weakest card as throwaway on unwinnable rounds
Cycles out duds → draw fresh replacements
Never waste a strong card on a lost cause

summary.title: Pick Two Mastery
bullets: Standard: setup card first, punchline second
Advanced: try deadpan anti-climax reversal
Match structure to current Czar's style

summary.title: When to Reboot
bullets: Reboot when: 0 playable cards + next Czar is readable
1 pt cost can net 2-3 pts over next several rounds
Don't reboot when behind and can't afford to lose a point

summary.title: Playing as the Underdog
bullets: Tables subconsciously punish the leader — use it
Behind: play confidently, let leader get overlooked
Ahead: play less obvious cards to hide your identity

summary.title: Anonymity Is Your Weapon
bullets: Vary submission timing — don't always go first or last
Poker face when Czar reads your answer
Let the answer stand on its own merit

summary.title: Endgame Awareness
bullets: Czars get reluctant to give leader the final point
Fly under radar as you approach the finish
Save universally strong cards for the final critical rounds

entries.definition: A prompt card with a fill-in-the-blank statement or question. The Card Czar draws and reads one each round. Some require one white card response, others require two or three.
An answer card with a noun, phrase, or concept. Players select these from their hand to fill in the blanks on the black card. Each player holds ten white cards at all times.
A black card that requires two white card responses, marked at the bottom. The order of submission determines the reading order. First card fills the first blank, second card fills the second.
A black card that requires three white card responses. Follows the same ordering rules as Pick Two. Less common but appears in some editions and expansion packs.

The player who draws and reads the black card, then judges all submissions and picks the winner. The Czar does not submit white cards during their judging round. The role rotates clockwise each round.
One point earned by winning a round. The winner keeps the black card as their point token. The player with the most Awesome Points at game end wins.
The white card or cards a player places face down in response to the black card prompt. Submissions are anonymous and shuffled before the Card Czar reads them aloud.

Each player maintains a hand of ten white cards. After submitting cards and the round is judged, players draw from the white deck to return to ten. The Card Czar does not draw during their judging round.
All submissions are placed face down and shuffled before reading. This ensures the Card Czar judges answers on humor alone, not on who played them. Players reveal themselves only if they win.
House rule where a random white card is drawn from the deck each round as an extra submission. If Rando's card wins, all human players lose the game. Adds unpredictability and a common enemy.
House rule allowing a player to trade in one Awesome Point to discard their entire hand and draw ten new white cards. Useful when your hand is full of unusable cards.
House rule that removes the Card Czar entirely. All players submit white cards, then everyone votes on the best combination. No player may vote for their own submission.
House rule allowing players to discard white cards they do not understand and draw replacements at any time with no penalty. Keeps the game accessible for all players.
House rule for ending the game. When the group is ready to stop, play the "Make a Haiku" black card as the final round. Gives the game a memorable sendoff.
House rule for Pick Two rounds. Each player draws an extra white card before submitting, temporarily bringing their hand to twelve. After the round, hands return to ten as normal.
